coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots derived from rice straw doped with N and S and its nanocomposites with hydroxypropyl cellulose nanocomposite
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Xuedi
Lotfy, Vivian F.
Basta, Altaf H.
Liu, Hao
Fu, Shiyu
coredata.dc:description: As biomass, rice straw (RS) is often valorized as a precursor of green products. In this respect, the RS-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are synthesized doped with N and S during the preparation. Synergistic doping with lipoic acid and ethylenediamine can vastly increase the yield of CQD from rice straw from 6.14 % to 62.8 %, and sulfur doping plays a more important role on the surface functional groups of the quantum dots. Further assessment is achieved toward the performance of SN-CQDs-hydroxypropyl cellulose nanocomposites. The optical behavior of synthesized SN-CQDs, and the critical concentration of its liquid crystal behaviors, at which the anisotropic phase begins to emerge, is approximately 1 %. Incorporating it into HPC, especially at 5 %, provided nanocomposite films with effective liquid crystal, tensile strength, and thermal stability. This sample's texture reveals a planar structure with colors ranging from yellow to red. The synergistic effect of incorporating SN-CQDs is shown by improving the strength to ~282.1 %, and the activation energy increased from 583.6 kJ.mol−1 to 615.1 kJ/mol. HPC-SN-CQDs can be assembled into an LED device, emitting warm light, of which CIE coordinate is (0.34,0.43).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Rice straw
Nanocomposites
Liquid crystal
Anisotropic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of S and N doped CQDs from rice straw
Preparation and characterization of HPC-nanocomposite films

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM pattern and particle size distribution of SN-CQDs; (b) TEM pattern and particle size distribution of N-CQDs; (c) TEM pattern of CQD with its interplanar spacing; (d) XRD pattern of SN-CQDs.
(a) FT-IR of SN-CQDs; (b) XPS survey; (c) C1s; (b) N1s; (d) S2p; (f) Si2p.
(a) UV–vis, PL, and PLE spectra of SN-CQDs solution, (I) under daylight; (II) under UV lighting; (b) PL emission spectra of SN-CQDs under different excitation light.
POM images of lyotropic suspension of CQD in water.
(a) 0.5 %, (b) 1 %, (c) 2 %, (d) 4 % and (e) 8 % (the vertical arrow is analyzer, and the horizontal arrow is polarizer).
POM images of HPC and HPC-nanocomposite films (a) HPC-1%SN-CQDs, (b) HPC-3%SN-CQDs, (c) HPC-5%SN-CQDs, (d) HPC, (e) HPC-5%SCB-CO ((d–e) reprinted with permission from Altaf H. Basta).
Cross-section morphology of films: (a–b) HPC; (c–d): HPC-1%SN-CQDS.
(a) I. Photos of HPC-1%SN-CQDs, II. LED device, (b) CIE coordinate of HPC-1%SN-CQDs.
Tensile strength property of HPC and HPC-nanocomposite films.
TGA/DTG analysis of HPC and HPC nanocomposite films.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots derived from rice straw doped with N and S and its nanocomposites with hydroxypropyl cellulose nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Lab. of Pulp and Paper Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Lab. of Pulp and Paper Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State State Key Lab. of Pulp and Paper Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, China 510640

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cellulose &Paper Dept., National Research Centre, El Buhouth St., Dokki, 12622 Giza, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Cellulose &Paper Dept., National Research Centre, El Buhouth St. Dokki-12622, Giza, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As biomass, rice straw (RS) is often valorized as a precursor of green products. In this respect, the RS-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are synthesized doped with N and S during the preparation. Synergistic doping with lipoic acid and ethylenediamine can vastly increase the yield of CQD from rice straw from 6.14 % to 62.8 %, and sulfur doping plays a more important role on the surface functional groups of the quantum dots. Further assessment is achieved toward the performance of SN-CQDs-hydroxypropyl cellulose nanocomposites. The optical behavior of synthesized SN-CQDs, and the critical concentration of its liquid crystal behaviors, at which the anisotropic phase begins to emerge, is approximately 1 %. Incorporating it into HPC, especially at 5 %, provided nanocomposite films with effective liquid crystal, tensile strength, and thermal stability. This sample's texture reveals a planar structure with colors ranging from yellow to red. The synergistic effect of incorporating SN-CQDs is shown by improving the strength to ~282.1 %, and the activation energy increased from 583.6 kJ.mol−1 to 615.1 kJ/mol. HPC-SN-CQDs can be assembled into an LED device, emitting warm light, of which CIE coordinate is (0.34,0.43).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the valency and diverse hybridization states (sp, sp2, sp3) of the carbon atom, it can form many allotropes such as diamond, fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, and carbon quantum dots. All these carbon nano allotropes are considered to belong to the same group, as its main component is a hexagonal network of sp2 carbon atoms. Parallel to the development of graphitic (sp2) species, carbon dots (C-dots) are another class of carbon nanostructures. Carbon dots contain combinations of sp3 and sp2 carbon atoms in various proportions and an amorphous polymer shell rich in O/N functional groups [1]. C-dots are amorphous, highly oxygenated, quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with sizes ranging from 2 to 10 nm. The most notable and distinctive feature of C-dots is their relatively strong photoluminescence, which is influenced by surface functionalization, excitation wavelength, and dot size [2]. Moreover, the functionalized surfaces give CDs many unique properties, such as good dispersion, adjustable photoluminescence, and easily modified electronic structure, etc., which makes it show significant application value in the fields of biological imaging [3], catalysis [4], optical devices [5] and environmental monitoring [6]. Approaches for preparing C-dots include the thermal decomposition or carbonization of organic compounds [7], solvothermal [8,9], or microwave treatment [10] of carbohydrates, polymers, organic compounds, organosilanes, or natural products. The most popular ways to produce C-dots are the hydrothermal and microwave approaches, which were thoroughly discussed in a review work by Yang et al. [11].

Carbon dots made from renewable biomass, such as plants, medicinal herbs, agricultural waste, and other organic biomaterials, are known as green CDs [12]. Since green preparation techniques are more cost-effective and environmentally friendly than chemical and physical processes, they are widely recommended. Furthermore, recycling waste materials into valuable products is an effective way to solve environmental problems [13–15]. Some of the utilized in the manufacturing of biomass CQDs are Auricularia auricula, green tea leaves, palm shell powder, banana peels, rubber seed shells and black liquor [16–21].

The directional features of novel carbon nanomaterials, which result from the exact orientation of graphene layers, cause the anisotropic behavior (building blocks) of all sp2 hybridized carbon forms and are largely responsible for the enthusiasm around them [22]. Based on covalent capture and template-directed liquid crystal (LC) assembly, four high-aspect ratio carbon nanomaterials have been developed [23]. It is prevailingly regarded that the current developments and future directions for the liquid crystal are focused on composites with dispersed nanomaterials [23–25]. A synergistic interaction between carbon-based nanomaterials and LCs has been observed for carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide, graphene fakes, graphene, and carbon dots. This interaction results in interesting properties like reduced ionic conductivity, faster-switching response, low switching threshold voltage, and the formation of localized pseudo-nematic domains [27]. Dalir and Javadian investigated LCs mixture from pentyl cyanobiphenyl (5CB) and heptyl cyanobiphenyl (7CB) LCs doped to form eutectic E5CN7 mixes using carboxyl functionalized single-walled carbon nanotubes [28].

Herein, the green carbon quantum dots synthesized from rice straw doped with sulfur and nitrogen were conducted to prepare liquid crystal materials and to improve the performance of hydroxypropyl cellulose (HPC) nanocomposite.

Rice straw (Hunan Province, China), ethylenediamine and lipoic acid (Aladdin). Hydroxypropyl cellulose [average Mw ~ 100,000, average degree of substitution (DS): 3.0, and average molar substitution (MS): 3.0] was purchased from sigma Aldrich.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of S and N doped CQDs from rice straw
ce:section.ce:para: Rice straw, ethylenediamine, and lipoic acid are used as carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur sources for hydrothermal method in one step to obtain SN-CQDs. The procedure is carried out as follows (Fig. 1 ): 0.4 g straw powder (60mesh) and 0.075 g lipoic acid were added to 30 mL deionized water with 125 μL ethylenediamine solution. The mixture was treated with ultrasonic generator for about 15 min, and then transferred to a 50mL high-pressure reactor lined with polytetrafluoroethylene to react at a preset temperature. After the reaction, the reactor was cooled to room temperature. The product was centrifuged, and the supernatant was filtered through the 0.22 μm filter membrane. The filtrate solution was transferred to a 1000 Da molecular weight dialysis bag for 48 h. The resulted reactants are freeze-dried to obtain brown quantum dot powder, of which aqueous solution glows bright blue under ultraviolet light.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of SN-CQDs powder was measured by an X'pert Powder Phaser diffractometer (Rigaku Smartlab 9KW, Japan), whose working voltage was 40 kV and the radiation source was Cu Κα (λ =1.0546 Å). The transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) patterns were derived from the JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope from JEOL. The photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were measured using a FluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrophotometer, whose excitation source was a 150 W xenon lamp. The X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) patterns were measured by K-Alpha X from Thermo Scientific company. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectra were obtained from Perkin Elmer's Lambda 950. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was measured by Nicolet IS50 from Thermo Scientific Company. Fluorescent lifetime spectra were measured by FLS1000 from Edinburgh Company.

Photomicrographs (POM) lyotropic suspension (0.5–8 wt./vol%) of SN-CQDs were recorded using a polarized light microscope. The sample was tested using an Olympus Polarizing Microscope model BX-53-P with Lamp housing U-LHLEDC100, Bertrand lens (UPLFLN-P SERIES) in a range of magnifications from 4× to 100×, rotatable analyzer (U-AN360P), condenser (U-POC-2) and Olympus cell Sens Software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of HPC-nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para:
50 % weight concentration HPC-nanocomposites were produced by dissolving HPC powder in water, either without or with the addition of 1, 3, and 5 % synthesized QDs. The prepared solutions were uniformly applied with an applicator on a glass plate, with the thickness adjusted to 0.2 mm, and allowed to air dry to produce films of consistent thickness. The following analyses were performed on the acquired films:
•
Photomicrographs (POM) of HPC-nanocomposite films were recorded using a polarized light microscope. The sample was tested using an Olympus Polarizing Microscope model BX-53-P with Lamp housing U-LHLEDC100, Bertrand lens (UPLFLN-P SERIES) in a range of magnifications from 4× to 100×, rotatable analyzer (U-AN360P), condenser (U-POC-2) and Olympus cellSens Software.
•
Mechanical properties of films (Tensile strength) were estimated according to ISO-527-1, 2012 [29] using universal testing machine at room temperature with a crosshead speed of 2 mm min−1 and gauge length of 30 mm. Each film composition was measured at least three times.
•
Thermo-gravimetric analysis (TGA and DTG) of the HPC-SN-CQDs nanocomposite is carried out using Setaram LABSYS EVO STA, France. The analysis is performed over a temperature range of 30–600 °C using a heating scan rate of 10 °C/min in an inert atmosphere of nitrogen gas (30 mL/min). The kinetic parameters of thermal degradation are calculated according to references.
•
The microstructure of the cross-section of the SN-CQDs HPC nanocomposite is measured by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi SU5000, Japan). The acceleration voltage is 3 kV and gold spraying are performed prior to the test.
•
The crystallinity of the composite films was determined by X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Smartlab 9KW, Japan). Test conditions: CuKα is the X-ray diffraction source, the electric voltage is 40 kV, the current is 40 mA, the scanning speed is 5°/min, the scanning range is 5–60°.

The sulfur and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (SN-CQDs) were prepared from rice straw by hydrothermal methods in one pot, as in Fig. 1. The prepared SN-CQDs were well dispersed in aqueous solution with an average particle size of 3.73 ± 0.78 nm (Fig. 2 (a)). The lattice striations are well defined as the space of 0.22 nm originating from the (100) plane of graphitic carbon in insert of Fig. 2(c). Typical diffraction peaks of typical amorphous carbon (Fig. 2(d)), which is also related to the (100) plane of graphite. Fig. 2(b) shows the N-CQDs obtained by N doping only under the same experimental conditions with an average particle size of 3.63 ± 0.75 nm, which is similar to that of the SN-CQDs.

The PLQY (Photoluminescence Quantum Yield, PLQY) of CQDs from rice straw synergically doping with sulfur (lipoic acid) and nitrogen (ethylenediamine) increased significantly from 6.14 % (no doping) to 62.8 %. When the ethylenediamine and lipoic acid were doped separately, the PLQY of each CQDs were 24.6 and 17.82 % (Table S1). The obtained data is consistent with the literature reports [30]. The great synergistic effect on PLQY is good for CODs preparation.

The reason for the improvement of PLQY can be explained as following: Firstly, surface passivation significantly affects the yield of biomass quantum dots. Doping S and N in the hydrothermal process can change the surface group and electron density of CQDs, which helps to regulate their PLQY to a higher level than without doping, so er can get high yield of CQDs in the present preparation process [32]. The functional groups and surface chemical composition components of CQDs were measured using FTIR, as shown in Fig. 3 (a). The broad band absorption at about 3400 cm−1 corresponds to the -OH and NH stretching vibrations, and the absorption peaks at 2940, 1645, 1400, and 1032 cm−1 belong to the CH, CO, CN, and C-O-C stretching vibrations, respectively [36].

Secondly, the synthesis method of biomass quantum dots will also affect their PLQYs. Specific synthesis methods can produce quantum dots with high fluorescence quantum yields. For example, carbon quantum dots can be obtained from straw powder under the nitrogen atmosphere at 300 °C, of which the yield of aqueous solution can be as high as 64 % [31]. Temperature and hydrothermal time are also affected. In this work, orthogonal experimental design was carried out for different factors in hydrothermal experiments (Table S2-S3). The optimum experimental conditions were determined to be 180 °C hydrothermal treatment for 8 h, and the molar ratio of ethylenediamine and lipoic acid was 5:1.

As a comparative experiment, when only ethylenediamine and lipoic acid were hydrothermal treated without straw as precursor, the PLQY was 21.24 % (Table S1), which was lower than that of the quantum dots obtained under the condition of only nitrogen doping, which could be attributed to the lack of carbon sources. This also reflects that straw as a precursor is also one of the important factors, because straw not only contains cellulose, lignin and other organic carbon elements, but also contains a high content of silicon elements. Silicon elements in the form of inorganic silica colloids (SiO2·nH2O) deposited in various organs. Combined with the XPS test data, we consider that the silicon element in the straw also contributes to the high yield of the quantum dots.

The total XPS spectrum of SN-CQDs is shown in Fig. 3(b). The peaks at 285.4, 532.9, and 400.4 eV belong to C1s, O 1 s, and N 1 s orbitals, respectively, and their percentage compositions are 67.71 %, 24.41 %, and 2.99 %. The C 1 s band is mainly divided into three peaks: 284.8, 286.0, and 287.7 eV, attributed to CC, C-O/C-N, and CO (Fig. 3(c)). The two fitted bands of N1s are 399.43 and 401.85 eV, attributed to C-N-C and NH, respectively (Fig. 3(d)); The deconvoluted high- resolution S 2p spectrum consisted of three peaks, in which peaks at 163.44 eV, 165.41 eV, and 167.91 eV were assigned to S 2p3/2, S 2p1/2 and SO respectively (Fig. 3(e)). Based on the FTIR and XPS analyses, the groups related to N, S and carbonation are present in SN-CQD [32]. Table S4 collected the FTIR surface function group and the XPS spectrum's peak deconvolution. In addition, since the rice straw possess large amount of biogenic silica with amorphous nature of hydrated SiO2 grains [33], the presence of Si elements is also detected in the prepared quantum dots (Fig. 3(f)).

In addition, research has shown that when other structures are similar, the proportion and content of silicon and nitrogen in the precursor have a great influence on the prepared CQDs. Without nitrogen, high-yield fluorescent CQDs cannot be synthesized. Therefore, appropriate silicon source to nitrogen content ratio and spatial structure are conducive to the formation of core and surface deactivation, thereby improving the PLQY [37].

The optical properties of SN-CQDs are shown in the UV–visible spectrum, spot spectrum, and PLE spectrum in Fig. 4 (a). Two shoulders at ~275 nm and ~ 340 nm can be found in the absorption spectra; the shoulder at 275 nm is from the π → π * transition of aromatic CC, and the n → π * transitions of aromatic CO and CN at 340 nm. The PLE spectra show a symmetric broadband peak at 350 nm, and the symmetric broadband peak appears in PL at a wavelength of 440 nm. The PLE spectrum shows a symmetric broadband peak at 350 nm, while the PL shows a symmetric broadband peak at 440 nm. In Fig. 4(b), the PL spectra of SN-CQDs represent intense excitation-dependent property, which originates from different surface groups; they make different surface states and edge states appear between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy bands [34].
Fig. 5 displays the thermal degradation (TGA and DTG) of SN-CQDs. The degradation temperature of SN-CQDs decomposes into four stages.

The breakdown process started around 30–100 °C, which corresponds to the removal of moisture in the QDs. The elimination of hydroxyl, carboxyl, nitro, and amide groups may have been the cause of the second breakdown stage that occurred between 200 and 350 °C, which had the most significant weight loss and DTG peak at 279.9 °C. The third stage of the decomposition occurred between 400 and 500 °C, which might have been brought about by the removal of phenyl groups. A plateau was reached when heated beyond 550 °C, and the last stage of decomposition happened around 500–600 °C, most likely because of the removal of pyridine and pyrrolidine groups [35]. These outcomes recommended the pureness and high thermal constancy of the SN-CQDs prepared from rice straw.

The solvent's liquid crystal (LC) concentration affects the formation of the lyotropic LC phase. The purpose of this study was to identify concentrations near the critical concentration via using POM to demonstrate the liquid crystal behavior of SN-CQDs in water (Fig. 6 ). The images under polarized light showed yellow bright with few dots at low concentrations (2 and 4 %) that got brighter with vast regions at 8 % when the concentration of SN-CQDs increased from 0.5 to 8 %. It is evident from the POM images that the critical concentration of doped CQDs at which the anisotropic phase begins to emerge is approximately 1 %. At concentrations below 1 %, the POM of SN-CQDs exhibited an isotropic phase.

Regarding the POM images (Fig. 7 (a–e)); the film of pure HPC dissolved previously in water solvent displays striated cholesteric spherulites [26]. The nanocomposite film from incorporating different amounts of SN-CQDs (1–5 %) to HPC showed that the incorporated QDs evolved these cholesteric spherulites of pure HPC to a characteristic texture under polarizing optical microscopy (POM). With increasing the ratio of SN-CQDs incorporated with HPC, the POM is more bright birefringence (Fig. 7(a–c)). In the case of HPC-5%SN-CQDs (Fig. 7(c)), an iridescent cellulose-based phase started to display. This sample's texture reveals a planar structure with colors ranging from yellow to red. When comparing the POM of the case study to our earlier research, a different carbon product made from sugar cane bagasse and HPC exhibited a POM that was roughly comparable to HPC's behavior, however with brighter birefringence patches, this is associated with the rectangular carbon oxide (Fig. 7(e)). This also suggests that the LC behavior of hydroxypropyl cellulose was positively impacted by the good synergistic action of the rice straw carbon quantum dot doped with N and S.

The mechanical properties of HPC films and other carbon materials with adding different amounts of SN-CQDs were studied. It can be seen from the data in Table 1 that the addition of SN-CQDs significantly improves the mechanical properties of HPC. Compared with HPC (2.25 ± 1.2 MPa), the tensile strength of HPC-nanocomposites is increased to 9.37 ± 1.36 MPa. The tensile strength of high-performance concrete with 1 % content increased by 316.4 %. The synergistic effect of adding SN-CQDs is attributed to the functional group of the nanoparticle, which may form a strong interaction with the functional group of HPC, thereby improving the mechanical properties. The mechanical strength of SN-CQDs nanocomposite film was compared with other carbon nanomaterials studied before, and it was found that the addition of SN-CQDs nanocomposite film had better mechanical strength. The tensile strength of HBC containing GO, SCB-CQDs and SCB-CO were 8.59 ± 1.6, 6.10 ± 1.4, 3.20 ± 0.57 and 1.78 ± 0.21 MPa, respectively. Again, this effective trend is consistent with the addition of graphene oxide and CQD to polyurethanes to improve the mechanical properties of the end product [14].

Fig. 8 shows the sectional morphology of HPC composite film. The pure HPC film has many small holes in Fig. 8(a–b), and the cross-section is smooth and discontinuous. The cross-section of HPC-SN-CQDs composite film shows apparent lamellar porosity and good compactness. However, the cross section of the composite film shows a uniform and dense microstructure, indicating that the addition of quantum dots enhances the link in HPC so that the mechanical strength of the composite film gains a lot.

In one primary stage of pyrolysis, the thermal decomposition curves (TGA and DTG) of HPC and its film with SN-CQDs were displayed in Fig. 9 , in comparison with other carbon nanoallotropes nanocomposite films based on graphene oxide (GO), bagasse-based carbon quantum dots (QD) and carbon oxide (CO) [14]. The TGA thermogram showed an increase in onset temperature (343.5 °C) in contrast to HPC (323.2 °C) and HPC with carbon nanoallotropes (324.7–232.4 °C). HPC with SN-CQDs begins to degrade at a higher temperature (343.5 °C). Maximum degradation temperatures (DTG peak) and the degradation stage's activation energy are essential factors that specify the higher thermal stability of doped carbon quantum dot nanocomposite films than HPC and HPC-other nanocomposite films. The same trend was noticed in the DTG peak temperature of HPC-SN-CQDs, where it is raised from 369.5 °C (HPC) to 372.0 °C, and for HPC-GO and HPC-SCB-CQD, it is raised to 373.7 °C and 370.5 °C, respectively. In the case of HPC-CO, the trend is inverted; the DTG peak temperature drops to 365.5 °C. Table 2 displays that the activation energies of HPC-SN-CQDs nanocomposites films are higher than those obtained from pure HPC film and other carbon nanoallotropes. The activation energy of degradation (Ea) was determined using the Coats and Redfern model and the least square method [13,38,39]. By comparison, HPC-SN-CQDs had an activation energy of 615.1 kJ/mol, while in the case of incorporating the bagasse-based carbon nanoallotropes (CO & CQDs), the activation energy of HPC film increases from 583.6 kJ.mol−1 to 615.1 kJ/mol The TGA measurements also proved the data of tensile strength.

To evaluate the behavior of the composite film in LED, the HPC-1%SN-CQDs was fixed on an LED device with the light of 365 nm to show the emitting light change caused by the CQD contained HPC film, in Fig. 8. The film is transparent so you can see the “SCUT” clearly underneath the film. It is yellowish in daylight and emits bright blue under ultraviolet (Fig. 10 (a) I). The LED device is constructed of a 365 nm UV light and composite film on top. It emits bright light under the power of a 3 V voltage (Fig. 10(a) II). In general, the CIE coordinate of white light is (0.33, 0.33). Here, the CIE coordinate of composite film is (0.34, 0.43) (Fig. 10(b)). The LED color rendering index (CRI) is 72 and color temperature is 5220 K, which means the emitted light belongs to warm light. The interesting point is that the composite film assembled LED device emit yellow-green light (Fig. S2), though the composite film itself fluoresces blue under ultraviolet light. This performance may be caused by the redshift of excited state energy of the quantum dots in thermally-oriented HPC film under the high temperature (~150 °C) and high excitation flux of the LED device [40]. On the other hand, the redshift may be accounted for the increase in spiral spaces of liquid crystal of HPC at high temperature.

This work highlighted the effective route for the valorization of rice straw (RS) as a precursor of green quantum dots, and further doped with S and N to form SN-CQDs as liquid crystal material. The prepared SN-CQDs in aqueous solution is in size of 3.73 ± 0.78 nm with PLQY of 62.8 %. The PLE and PL appeared at the wavelength of 350 nm and 440 nm, respectively. The critical concentration of its liquid crystal behaviors is approximately 1 %, at which the anisotropic phase begins to emerge. The SN-CQDs had a synergetic effect on the performance of HPC-nanocomposite film, and 5 % SN-CQDs in HPC provided the texture of planar structure with colors ranging from yellow to red. In addition, it provided improvement in the strength to ~282.1 % and thermal stability (activation energy increased from 583.6 kJ.mol−1 to 615.1 kJ/mol). The SN-CQDs HPC composite film assembled on LEDs device can produce warm light, which may provide a potential to manufacture high-efficient LEDs devices to be benefit to eyes of human-being.

Xuedi Yang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Vivian F. Lotfy: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Altaf H. Basta: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hao Liu: Investigation. Shiyu Fu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was carried out under the grant Egypt-China Cooperation Program: funded by international project of Chinese SKRD Program with No: 2021YFE0104500 and Science, Technology, and Innovation Funding Authority with Contact/Agreement No. 43068.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2024.134925.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Broad family of carbon nanoallotropes: classification, chemistry, and applications of fullerenes, carbon dots, nanotubes, graphene, nanodiamonds, and combined superstructures

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Georgakilas, J. A. Perman, J. Tucek, and R. Zboril, Broad Family of Carbon Nanoallotropes: Classification, Chemistry, and Applications of Fullerenes, Carbon Dots, Nanotubes, Graphene, Nanodiamonds, and Combined Superstructures, Chemical Reviews, 115(11), (2015), pp. 4744–4822, doi:10.1021/cr500304f.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang and A. Hu, Carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications, Journal of Materials Chemistry C, 2(34), (2014), pp. 6921-6939, doi:10.1039/c4tc00988f.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green and continuous microflow synthesis of fluorescent carbon quantum dots for bio-imaging application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Lin, X. Yuan, H. Wen, W. Lu, H. Xu, and J. Zhou, Green and Continuous Microflow Synthesis of Fluorescent Carbon Quantum Dots for Bio-imaging Application, AIChE Journal, 69(1), (2022), Article e17901, doi:10.1002/aic.17901.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Amine-functionalized carbon nanodot electrocatalysts converting carbon dioxide to methane
